items.title: Venezuela beats U.S. to win country's 1st World Baseball Classic title
items.summary_auto:
Venezuela won the World Baseball Classic for the first time Tuesday night, beating the United States 3-2 in the championship
game on Eugenio Suarez's tiebreaking double in the top of the ninth inning.

items.title: Two Blocks from the White House: Trump’s coalition of the pressured
items.summary_auto: President Donald Trump is sending mixed messages around his demands of U.S. allies when it comes to defending the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Did organized criminals breach the Vancouver police property office? Court docs reveal VPD investigation
items.summary_auto: CBC News has learned Vancouver police are investigating whether someone with access to the force's property office colluded with organized criminals to tamper with key evidence in a high-profile murder case.

items.title: Sask. medical clinics impose fines, bans for no-show patients
items.summary_auto: Dr. Jason Hosain, who has studied no-shows in Saskatchewan and the response to them, said he understands the increased value placed on appointments with family doctors, even though he does not believe no-shows represent a growing problem.

items.title: Iran launches fresh attacks on Israel, Gulf neighbours after 2 high-ranking leaders killed
items.summary_auto: Iran launched a new wave of strikes following the killing of two high-ranking leaders in an airstrike with attacks on its Persian Gulf neighbours and Israel on Wednesday, using some of its latest missiles to evade air defences and killing two people near Tel Aviv, as the war in the Middle East showed no signs of slowing.

items.title: New program aims to support and connect 2SLGBTQ+ entrepreneurs in Canada
items.summary_auto: The Canadian Queer Chamber of Commerce has launched the FAM Program. It's aimed at providing networking, training and other supports to 2SLGBTQ+ entrepreneurs and businesses.

items.title: Israeli airstrikes kill 10 in central Beirut, Lebanese authorities say
items.summary_auto: More than 900 people have been ​killed in Lebanon by Israeli attacks since Hezbollah entered the regional war in support of Tehran on March 2, and more than 1 ⁠million people have been forced to flee their homes, Lebanese authorities say.

items.title: As gun crime rises in N.L., so do efforts by police and fears for innocent civilians
items.summary_auto: Police in Newfoundland and Labrador are seizing more guns than ever before. New statistics obtained by CBC News tell the story.

items.title: Border agency 'systemic collapse' allows man found guilty of immigration fraud to walk free and sue Canada
items.summary_auto: Gurpreet Singh was found responsible for running an immigration fraud scheme in Saskatchewan, but a judge granted him a “windfall” — no criminal record and no jail sentence. Now Singh is suing the government and some employees of the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA), alleging they violated his charter rights.

items.title: Canadian crypto fugitive is seeking U.S. presidential pardon on criminal indictment, U.S. lobbying firm says
items.summary_auto: A Washington, D.C., lobbying firm says it is seeking a presidential pardon for Canadian crypto fugitive Andean Medjedovic, documents filed with the U.S. Department of Justice show.

items.title: Self-harm among young Canadians is on the rise, specifically in girls, new research finds
items.summary_auto: A growing number of young people are turning to self-harm in Canada and other parts of the world, according to new research.

items.title: Northwestern Ontario pleads for federal funding as highway fatalities mount
items.summary_auto: Northern Ontario politicians are urgently appealing to the federal government for help during one of the deadliest winters on record along 2,000 kilometres of highway between Nipigon, Sudbury and North Bay.

items.title: Top-ranked UBC looks to turn dominant season into 1st women's hockey championship
items.summary_auto: The University of British Columbia is the top seed among the eight teams vying for a national Canadian university women's hockey championship, which begins on Thursday.

items.title: As Niagara Region’s only safe drug consumption site faces closure, advocates fear more people will die
items.summary_auto: Advocates fear the looming closure of the Niagara Region's sole safe drug consumption site, in St. Catharines, Ont., will result in a spike in overdose deaths and cases of blood-borne infections such as hepatitis C and HIV.

items.title: Thunder Bay, Ont., long-term care home, staff member face criminal charges in resident's 2025 death
items.summary_auto: A city-operated long-term care facility in Thunder Bay, Ont., and a staff member face criminal charges following the death of an 86-year-old resident. Here's what we know about the investigation.

items.title: 'We've had enough secrets': First Nations group opposes Ontario move to limit freedom-of-information laws
items.summary_auto: A group representing First Nations is speaking out against Ontario’s proposed legislation to change freedom-of-information laws, saying there's concern the move would reduce Indigenous communities’ ability to access important information.

items.title: As gas prices rise, ride-hail drivers feel especially pinched at the pump
items.summary_auto: Gas prices worldwide have soared  since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran, and the widening war has pinched tanker traffic in the crucially-important Strait of Hormuz shipping route. Ride-hail drivers, many of whom are on the road for hours a day, say they're being hit hard by the extra expense.

items.title: Diesel prices surge to highest since 2022, raising concern for shipping, consumer costs
items.summary_auto: The ongoing U.S.-Iran war is causing diesel prices to spike across the country, climbing nearly 30 per cent since the beginning of March. In some parts of Quebec, a litre of diesel is selling for almost $2.50.

items.title: P.E.I. MLA and cabinet minister Mark McLane has died
items.summary_auto: P.E.I. Progressive Conservative MLA and cabinet minister Mark McLane has died, with Premier Rob Lantz paying tribute to his colleague as someone who could always be counted upon.

items.title: #TheMoment he made ice cream while running in Calgary
items.summary_auto: Jonny Arnott tells The National about the moment he churned ice cream while on a run in Calgary.

items.title: Lululemon's net income down as brand promises product shakeup
items.summary_auto: Interim co-chief executive and chief financial officer Meghan Frank said Tuesday that the apparel retailer plans to shake up its product assortment with fewer logos, a "more focused and co-ordinated" colour palette and a "more edited" assortment of small accessories.

items.title: B.C. premier signals he won't support feds' temporary foreign worker program expansion
items.summary_auto: B.C. Premier David Eby has signalled he won't support the federal government's move to temporarily increase rural employers' allowances for temporary foreign workers, saying there should be a pathway to permanent residency instead.

items.title: Netanyahu posts video to dispel rumours of his death after disinformation spreads online
items.summary_auto: ​Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu posted a video of himself getting a coffee on Sunday in an apparent attempt to prove that online rumours of his death have been greatly exaggerated.

items.title: Cuba reconnects electrical grid, but millions are still without power
items.summary_auto: Cuba has reconnected its electrical grid across much of the country, the Energy and Mines Ministry said early on Tuesday, just hours after U.S. President Donald Trump escalated his rhetoric against the communist-run ‌island, saying he could do anything he wanted with Cuba.

items.title: Longest ballot organizers eye protest in upcoming Montreal-area byelection
items.summary_auto: A group that has signed up droves of protest candidates in a number of elections and byelections in recent years is organizing in another upcoming vote, this time in Terrebonne.

items.title: Is Iran waging war with mines in the Strait of Hormuz? | About That
items.summary_auto: Andrew Chang explains what we know about Iran's sea mine capabilities and why they can be such a destructive and difficult naval threat to remove — especially in a critical shipping route. Images provided by The Canadian Press, Reuters, Adobe Stock, CAT-UXO and Getty Images

items.title: 4-day work weeks, rationing, dressing down: How some Asian countries are coping with the Iran oil crisis
items.summary_auto: Much of the oil produced in the Middle East is exported to Asian countries, which are now using a range of different methods to adjust to the fuel crunch caused by Iran's partial blockage of the Strait of Hormuz amid its war with the U.S. and Israel.

items.title: Doug Armstrong steps down as general manager of Canada's Olympic hockey team
items.summary_auto: Doug Armstrong is stepping down from his position as general manager of Canada's Olympic hockey team. Hockey Canada says Armstrong informed the organization he will be focusing on his position with the St. Louis Blues going forward.

items.title: Police anticipated 'worst-case scenario' by deploying sniper at St. Patrick's Day party, says former officer
items.summary_auto: The sighting of a sniper on Saturday during St. Patrick’s Day celebrations in Waterloo was met with fear and shock by some, but one policing expert from Wilfrid Laurier University says this is a proactive measure designed to keep the crowd safe.

items.title: Federal government appeals Emergencies Act use during convoy protest to Supreme Court
items.summary_auto: The federal government is bringing its case to justify use of the Emergencies Act to clear the convoy protests that gridlocked the capital city and border points to the country's highest court.

items.title: Should we worry about Canada's winter sports decline?
items.summary_auto: Canada's Winter Olympic and Paralympic medal counts are falling. Is this the sign of a crisis, or merely a shift?

items.title: IN PHOTOS | Celebrating St. Patrick's Day in Canada and abroad
items.summary_auto: St. Patrick's Day is marked annually on March 17, the traditional death date of Ireland's patron saint, and has become a celebration of Irish culture and heritage worldwide.

items.title: The world's oldest-known whale song recordings tell a story about the changing ocean
items.summary_auto: Researchers at Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution have discovered a recording of humpback whales off the coast of Bermuda in March 1949 buried in their archives, marking the oldest-known recording of whale song.

items.title: The Strait of Hormuz kill box | About That
items.summary_auto: Andrew Chang explains why unblocking the world's most vital energy chokepoint is much harder than it may seem. Images provided by The Canadian Press, Reuters and Getty Images

items.title: 23 passengers booted from Saturday night Halifax-to-Cancun flight
items.summary_auto: Julia Kaiser, a spokesperson for WestJet, says an unexpected cabin crew absence meant that according to federal regulations, the number of passengers had to be cut to 150. There were 173 passengers ready to board.

items.title: Swapping stones led to Canada's 5th straight win at women's curling worlds
items.summary_auto: Canada's fifth straight win at the women's world curling championship was a lesson in adaptation for skip Kerri Einarson.

items.title: Former Bloc Québécois MP charged with sex crimes involving minors
items.summary_auto: The alleged incidents date back to 2006  — before Pascal-Pierre Paillé's election to the House of Commons — and as recently as August 2024.

items.title: Doctor Who fans get to step back in time with the discovery of 2 long-lost episodes
items.summary_auto: For decades, dozens of early Doctor Who episodes were feared to be lost forever — until two of them turned up recently in the garage of a deceased collector.

items.title: International Olympic Committee urged to drop reported gender test plans for female athletes
items.summary_auto: More than 80 human rights and sport advocacy groups have called on the ‌International Olympic Committee to abandon reported plans to introduce universal genetic sex testing for female athletes and impose a blanket ban on transgender and intersex competitors.

items.title: Has Banksy’s true identity finally been revealed?
items.summary_auto: After decades of mystery, an investigation by Reuters, spanning from Ukraine to New York, may have just revealed the street artist Banksy’s identity. Reporter Blake Morrision, talks about his investigation and if knowing who the artist is changes our relationship with his work.

items.title: Fisheries officials investigating video of sailors bragging about giant crab catch near Vancouver
items.summary_auto: Parts of Vancouver's harbour are off-limits to crabbing, and while the men say they have stopped in "Vancouver, Canada," it's not clear exactly where the vessel is anchored.
